name: Mock Test - 02
instruction: The Question Paper contains three sections. Section A has 25 questions. Attempt any 20 questions. Section B has 24 questions. Attempt any20 questions. Section C has 6 questions. Attempt any 5 questions. All questions carry equal marks. There is no negative marking.

questions.question_text: A long solid non-conducting cylinder of radius R is charged such that the volume charge density is proportional to r, where r is the distance from the axis. The electric field E at a distance r(r < R) will depend on r as:
questions.question_html: A long solid non-conducting cylinder of radius R is charged such that the volume charge density is proportional to r, where r is the distance from the axis. The electric field E at a distance r(r < R) will depend on r as:
questions.explanation: {tex}\rho \propto r \Rightarrow \rho=\alpha r{/tex} dp = {tex}\rho{/tex} dV = {tex}\alpha{/tex} r {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} dr {tex}\times{/tex} l Using Guass's theorem, E {tex}\times2 \pi r l=\frac{\int_{0}^{r} 2 \pi \alpha l r^{2} d r}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{2 \pi \alpha l r^{3}}{3 \varepsilon_{0} \times 2 \pi r l}=\frac{\alpha r^{2}}{3 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} E {tex}\propto{/tex} r 2

questions.question_text: In bringing an electron towards another electron, the electrostatic potential energy of the system
questions.question_html: In bringing an electron towards another electron, the electrostatic potential energy of the system
questions.explanation: P.E of a system is defined as work done in bringing a system of charges from infinity to a particular point. {tex}U=\frac{q_1q_2}{4\pi\in_0\;r}{/tex} So, as the separation among charges decreases, the potential energy of the system will increase.

questions.question_text: If the number of turns, area and current through a coil is given by n, A and I respectively, then its magnetic moment will be:
questions.question_html: If the number of turns, area and current through a coil is given by n, A and I respectively, then its magnetic moment will be:
questions.explanation: Magnetic moment is defined as the product of total current and area of loop {tex}M=n\times I\times A{/tex}

questions.question_text: Conservation of charges in tribo electric charging
questions.question_html: Conservation of charges in tribo electric charging

options.text: implies one is positively charged and the other is negatively charged
options.html: implies one is positively charged and the other is negatively charged

options.text: implies half are positively charged and other half are neutral
options.html: implies half are positively charged and other half are neutral

questions.correct_option_text: implies one is positively charged and the other is negatively charged
questions.explanation: implies one is positively charged and the other is negatively charged.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is correct for energy of a dipole in a uniform electric field?
questions.question_html: Which of the following is correct for energy of a dipole in a uniform electric field?

options.text: Dipole aligns along electric field and its energy decreases
options.html: Dipole aligns along electric field and its energy decreases

options.text: It only aligns itself along the electric field
options.html: It only aligns itself along the electric field

questions.correct_option_text: Dipole aligns along electric field and its energy decreases
questions.explanation: The potential energy of an electric dipole at any point in uniform electric field is {tex}U=-pE\cos\theta{/tex} When the dipole aligns itself along electric field, it gains low energy state during this process due to which its energy decreases.

questions.question_text: For a cell, the terminal difference is 2.2 V, when the circuit is open and reduces to 1.8 V, when cell is connected to a resistance R = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . The internal resistance of cell r is:
questions.question_html: For a cell, the terminal difference is 2.2 V, when the circuit is open and reduces to 1.8 V, when cell is connected to a resistance R = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. The internal resistance of cell r is:
questions.explanation: Internal resistance of the cell is r = {tex}\left(\frac{E \ - \ V}{V}\right) R=\left(\frac{2.2 \ - \ 1 \cdot 8}{1.8}\right) 5 \Omega=\frac{10}{9} \Omega{/tex}

questions.question_text: Lenz's law is a consequence of the law of conservation of
questions.question_html: Lenz's law is a consequence of the law of conservation of
questions.explanation: Lenz's law is a consequence of the law of conservation of energy. Lenz's law says that induced current always tends to oppose the cause which produces it. So work is done against opposing force. This work is transformed into electrical energy. So, it a consequence of law of conservation of energy.

questions.question_text: If the inclination is {tex}\theta{/tex} , the horizontal component of the earth’s field is H E and the vertical component of the earth’s field is Z E , then tan( {tex}\theta{/tex} ) is
questions.question_html: If the inclination is {tex}\theta{/tex}, the horizontal component of the earth’s field is HE and the vertical component of the earth’s field is ZE, then tan({tex}\theta{/tex}) is
questions.explanation: {tex}Z_E=B\sin\theta{/tex} {tex}H_E=B\cos\theta{/tex} Dividing the two we get, {tex}tan\theta=\frac{Z_E}{H_E}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Two concentric circular coils, one of small radius {tex}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{1}}}{/tex} and the other of large radius {tex}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}{/tex} , such that {tex}{\rm{r_1 }} < < {\rm{ r_2}}{/tex} , are placed co-axially with centres coinciding. Mutual inductance of the arrangement is
questions.question_html: Two concentric circular coils, one of small radius {tex}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{1}}}{/tex} and the other of large radius {tex}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}{/tex}, such that {tex}{\rm{r_1 }} < < {\rm{ r_2}}{/tex}, are placed co-axially with centres coinciding. Mutual inductance of the arrangement is
questions.explanation: Let a time varying current I 2 flow through the outer circular coil. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Magnetic field at the centre of this coil is {tex}B_2=\frac{\mu_0 I_2}{2 r_2}{/tex} Since the inner coil placed co-axially has very small radius, B 2 may be considered constant over its cross-sectional area. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Magnetic flux associated with inner coil is {tex}\phi_{1}=B_{2} \times \pi r_{1}^{2}=\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I_{2}}{2 r_{2}}\right) \pi r_{1}^{2}=\pi r_{1}^{2} \frac{\mu_{0} I_{2}}{2 r_{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\phi_{1}=\left(\frac{\mu_{0} \pi r_{1}^{2}}{2 r_{2}}\right) I_{2}=M_{12} I_{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}M_{12}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi r_{1}^{2}}{2 r_{2}}{/tex} Now, {tex}M_{12}=M_{21}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi r_{1}^{2}}{2 r_{2}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: For the LR-circuit shown in the figure the phase angle, if the frequency is f = {tex}\frac{100}{\pi}{/tex} ,
questions.question_html: For the LR-circuit shown in the figure the phase angle, if the frequency is f = {tex}\frac{100}{\pi}{/tex},
questions.explanation: {tex}\tan \phi=\frac{X_{L}}{R}=\frac{\omega L}{R}=\frac{2 \pi\left(\frac{100}{\pi}\right) \times 0.025}{5}=1{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\phi{/tex} = 45 o

questions.question_text: An electron having charge 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 C and mass 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 kg is moving with 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 m/s speed in a magnetic field of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -1 tesla in a circular orbit. The force acting on an electron and the radius of circular orbit will be
questions.question_html: An electron having charge 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C and mass 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg is moving with 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 m/s speed in a magnetic field of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-1 tesla in a circular orbit. The force acting on an electron and the radius of circular orbit will be
questions.explanation: {tex}F=e v B=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 4 \times 10^{6} \times 2 \times 10^{-1}{/tex} = 1.28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -13 N {tex}r=\frac{m v}{e B}=\frac{9 \times 10^{-31} \times 4 \times 10^{6}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2 \times 10^{-1}}{/tex} =1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m

questions.question_text: One kilowatt-hour is equal to:
questions.question_html: One kilowatt-hour is equal to:
questions.explanation: 1 kWh = 1000 W {tex}\times{/tex} 3600 s = 36 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 J

If the tapping key is pressed and released the galvaometer
questions.question_html: Coils {tex}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{1}}}{/tex} and {tex}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{/tex} are stationary. {tex}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{1}}}{/tex} connected in series with a galvanometer while {tex}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{/tex} is connected to a battery through a tapping key. If the tapping key is pressed and released the galvaometer

options.text: shows deflection while current in {tex}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{/tex} is changing
options.html: shows deflection while current in {tex}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{/tex} is changing

questions.correct_option_text: shows deflection while current in {tex}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{/tex} is changing
questions.explanation: flux will be changed when current in C 2 is changing. Hence current will be induced in coil C 1 .

questions.question_text: The wiring for a refrigerator contains a starter capacitor. A voltage of amplitude 170 V and frequency 60 Hz applied across the capacitor is to produce a current amplitude of 0.850 A through the capacitor. Capacitance required is
questions.question_html: The wiring for a refrigerator contains a starter capacitor. A voltage of amplitude 170 V and frequency 60 Hz applied across the capacitor is to produce a current amplitude of 0.850 A through the capacitor. Capacitance required is
questions.explanation: V = 170 volt, f = 60 Hz, i = 0.85 A {tex}V = i{X_C} = i\frac{1}{{\omega C}} = \frac{i}{{2\pi fC}}{/tex} Thus, Capacitance required, {tex}C = {i \over {2\pi fV}} = {{0.85} \over {2 \times 3.14 \times 60 \times 170}} = 13.3 \times {10^{ - 6}}F = 13.3\mu F{/tex}

questions.question_text: A conducting sphere of radius 10 cm has an unknown charge. If the electric field 20 cm from the centre of the sphere is {tex}{\rm{1}}.{\rm{5 }} \times {\rm{ 1}}{0^{\rm{3}}}{\rm{N}}/{\rm{C}}{/tex} and points radially inward, what is the net charge on the sphere?
questions.question_html: A conducting sphere of radius 10 cm has an unknown charge. If the electric field 20 cm from the centre of the sphere is {tex}{\rm{1}}.{\rm{5 }} \times {\rm{ 1}}{0^{\rm{3}}}{\rm{N}}/{\rm{C}}{/tex} and points radially inward, what is the net charge on the sphere?
questions.explanation: {tex}{\text{E = }}\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{q}{{{r^2}}}{/tex} {tex}1.5 \times {10^3} = \frac{{9 \times {{10}^9}q}}{{{{\left( {20 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} So, net charge is given by :- {tex}q = 6.67 \times {10^{ - 9}}C = 6.67nC{/tex} Since the electric field is inwards so charge is negative.

questions.question_text: A capacitor of capacitance C 1 is charged upto potential V and then connected in parallel to an uncharged capacitor of capacitance C 2 . The final potential difference across each capacitor will be:
questions.question_html: A capacitor of capacitance C1 is charged upto potential V and then connected in parallel to an uncharged capacitor of capacitance C2. The final potential difference across each capacitor will be:
questions.explanation: The common potential difference across the parallel combination of two capacitors, {tex}V^{\prime}=\frac{C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}{/tex} But V 1 = V, V 2 = 0 {tex}\therefore V^{\prime}=\frac{C_{1} V}{C_{1}+C_{2}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A ring of radius r is uniformly charged with charge q. It is rotated with angular velocity ω, then magnetic moment developed by the ring is
questions.question_html: A ring of radius r is uniformly charged with charge q. It is rotated with angular velocity ω, then magnetic moment developed by the ring is
questions.explanation: The magnetic moment of the ring is m = IA. The current, {tex}I = \frac{q}{T}{/tex} where, T is the time taken by the ring to complete one rotation. Since, {tex}T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega }{/tex} where {tex}\omega {/tex} is the angular speed of the ring {tex}I = \frac{q}{T} = \frac{{q\omega }}{{2\pi }}{/tex} The area covered by the ring due to its rotation, {tex}A = \pi {r^2}{/tex} Thus, {tex}m = IA = \frac{{q\omega }}{{2\pi }} \times \pi {r^2} = \frac{{q\omega {r^2}}}{2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A coil of inductance 0.50 H and resistance 100 {tex}\Omega {/tex} is connected to a 240 V, 50 Hz ac supply. Maximum current in the coil and time lag between the voltage maximum and the current maximum are
questions.question_html: A coil of inductance 0.50 H and resistance 100 {tex}\Omega {/tex} is connected to a 240 V, 50 Hz ac supply. Maximum current in the coil and time lag between the voltage maximum and the current maximum are
questions.explanation: L = 0.5H {tex}R = 100\Omega{/tex} V = 240 volt f = 50Hz Peak voltage, {tex}{V_0} = V\sqrt 2 = 240\sqrt 2 = 339.41volt{/tex} Angular frequency, {tex}\omega = 2\pi f = 2 \times 3.14 \times 50 = 314rad/\sec {/tex} Maximum current in circuit, {tex}{i_0} = {{{V_0}} \over {\sqrt {{R^2} + {\omega ^2}{L^2}} }} = {{339.41} \over {\sqrt {{{\left( {100} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {314} \right)}^2}{{\left( {0.5} \right)}^2}} }} = 1.82A{/tex} {tex}\tan \phi = \frac{{\omega L}}{R} = \frac{{314 \times 0.5}}{{100}} = 1.57{/tex} {tex}\phi = 57.5^\circ = \frac{{57.5\pi }}{{180}}rad{/tex} Now, {tex}\phi = \omega t{/tex} {tex}\frac{{57.5\pi }}{{180}} = 314t{/tex} {tex}t = \frac{{57.5 \times 3.14}}{{180 \times 314}} = 3.19 \times {10^{ - 3}}s = 3.2ms{/tex}

questions.question_text: A diamagnetic material in a magnetic field moves
questions.question_html: A diamagnetic material in a magnetic field moves
questions.explanation: When a diamagnetic substance is placed in the magnetic field produced by a magnet, its atoms acquire a small magnetic dipole moment in a direction opposite to that of the magnet. As a result, the diamagnetic substance moves slowly from stronger part of the magnetic field to the weaker part i.e. it is feebly repelled by the magnet.

questions.question_text: How many 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitors must be connected in parallel to store a charge of 1C with a potential of 110 V across the capacitors?
questions.question_html: How many 1{tex}\mu{/tex}F capacitors must be connected in parallel to store a charge of 1C with a potential of 110 V across the capacitors?
questions.explanation: Q = Q 1 + Q 2 + Q 3 + ... Q n = nC {tex}\times{/tex} V {tex}n=\frac{Q}{C V}=\frac{1 C}{1 \mu F \times 110 V}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{10^{6}}{110}{/tex} = 9090

questions.question_text: An electric field can deflect
questions.question_html: An electric field can deflect
questions.explanation: Alpha particles (nucelus of helium atom) are charged particles so they will be affected by electric field. They will get deflected in direction of electric field as they are positively charged.

questions.question_text: An inductor of reactance 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and a resistor of 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} are connected in series to the terminals of a 6 V (rms) a.c. source. The power dissipated in the circuit is
questions.question_html: An inductor of reactance 1{tex}\Omega{/tex} and a resistor of 2{tex}\Omega{/tex} are connected in series to the terminals of a 6 V (rms) a.c. source. The power dissipated in the circuit is
questions.explanation: According to the problem, X L = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , R = 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , Erms = 6V, P av = ? Average power dissipated in the circuit {tex}P_{\mathrm{av}}=E_{\mathrm{rms}} I_{\mathrm{rms}} \cos \phi{/tex} ..... (i) {tex}I_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{E_{\mathrm{rms}}}{Z}{/tex} impedence of the circuit is given by:- {tex}Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+X_{L}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{4+1}=\sqrt{5}{/tex} {tex}I_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{6}{\sqrt{5}} \mathrm{A}{/tex} {tex}\cos \phi=\frac{R}{Z}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}{/tex} {tex}P_{\mathrm{av}}=6 \times \frac{6}{\sqrt{5}} \times \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}{/tex} [from eq. (i)] {tex}=\frac{72}{\sqrt{5} \sqrt{5}}=\frac{72}{5}=14.4 \mathrm{W}{/tex}

questions.question_text: What should be the core of an electromagnet?
questions.question_html: What should be the core of an electromagnet?
questions.explanation: soft iron

questions.question_text: An electron moves in a circular orbit with a uniform speed v. It produces a magnetic field B at the centre of the circle. The radius of the circle is proportional to:
questions.question_html: An electron moves in a circular orbit with a uniform speed v. It produces a magnetic field B at the centre of the circle. The radius of the circle is proportional to:
questions.explanation: Here T = {tex}\frac{2 \pi R}{v}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{q}{T}=\frac{q v}{2 \pi R}{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 R} \cdot \frac{q v}{2 \pi R}=\frac{\mu_{0} q v}{4 \pi R^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}R^{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} q v}{4 \pi B}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}R \propto \sqrt{\frac{v}{B}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A galvanometer having a coil resistance of 60 {tex}\Omega{/tex} shows full-scale deflection when a current of 1.0 amp passes through it. It can be converted into an ammeter to read currents up to 5.0 amp by:
questions.question_html: A galvanometer having a coil resistance of 60{tex}\Omega{/tex} shows full-scale deflection when a current of 1.0 amp passes through it. It can be converted into an ammeter to read currents up to 5.0 amp by:

options.text: putting in parallel a resistance of 240 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
options.html: putting in parallel a resistance of 240{tex}\Omega{/tex}

options.text: putting in series a resistance of 240 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
options.html: putting in series a resistance of 240{tex}\Omega{/tex}

options.text: putting in series a resistance of 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
options.html: putting in series a resistance of 15{tex}\Omega{/tex}

options.text: putting in parallel a resistance of 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
options.html: putting in parallel a resistance of 15{tex}\Omega{/tex}

questions.correct_option_text: putting in parallel a resistance of 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
questions.explanation: {tex}S=\frac{I_{g} G}{I-I_{g}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1 \times 60}{5-1}=15 \Omega{/tex} , in parallel

questions.question_text: For a single turn circular coil of dipole moment m, the magnetic field on the axis x >> R (distance x and radius R) is
questions.question_html: For a single turn circular coil of dipole moment m, the magnetic field on the axis x >> R (distance x and radius R) is
questions.explanation: The magnetic field at a point distance x from a single turn circular coil of radius R carrying current I is given by {tex}B = \frac{{{\mu _0}I{R^2}}}{{2{{\left( {{R^2} + {x^2}} \right)}^{\frac{3}{2}}}}}{/tex} For x >> R, R can be neglected in the denominator. {tex}B = \frac{{{\mu _0}I{R^2}}}{{2{x^3}}}{/tex} Multiplying numerator and denominator by {tex}\pi {/tex} , we get {tex}B = \frac{{{\mu _0}I\left( {\pi {R^2}} \right)}}{{2\pi {x^3}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}IA}}{{2\pi {x^3}}}{/tex} as A = {tex}\pi {R^2}{/tex} Since the magnetic moment of a current carrying coil is m = IA Therefore, {tex}B = \frac{{{\mu _0}m}}{{2\pi {x^3}}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A 40 F capacitor in a defibrillator is charged to 3000 V. The energy stored in the capacitor is sent through the patient during a pulse of duration 2 ms. The power delivered to the patient is
questions.question_html: A 40 F capacitor in a defibrillator is charged to 3000 V. The energy stored in the capacitor is sent through the patient during a pulse of duration 2 ms. The power delivered to the patient is
questions.explanation: {tex}P=\frac{W}{t}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}}{t}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times\left(40 \times 10^{-6}\right) \times(3000)^{2}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \mathrm{~W}{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 W = 90 kW

questions.question_text: A conducting sphere of radius 5 cm is charged to 15 {tex}\mu {/tex} C. Another uncharged sphere of radius 10 cm is allowed to touch it for enough time. After the two are separated, the surface density of charge on the two spheres will be in the ratio
questions.question_html: A conducting sphere of radius 5 cm is charged to 15 {tex}\mu {/tex}C. Another uncharged sphere of radius 10 cm is allowed to touch it for enough time. After the two are separated, the surface density of charge on the two spheres will be in the ratio

questions.question_text: If C and L denote the capacitance and inductance respectively, the units of LC are
questions.question_html: If C and L denote the capacitance and inductance respectively, the units of LC are
questions.explanation: {tex}f=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{L C}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow L C=\frac{1}{4 \pi^{2} f^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [LC] = [M 0 L 0 T 2 ]

questions.question_text: If number of turns per unit length of a coil of a solenoid is doubled, its self-inductance will
questions.question_html: If number of turns per unit length of a coil of a solenoid is doubled, its self-inductance will
questions.explanation: {tex}L=\mu_{0} n^{2}{/tex} Al i.e., {tex}\mu \propto n^{2}{/tex} When n is doubled, L becomes four times its initial value.

questions.question_text: A closely wound solenoid of 800 turns and area of cross section {tex}{\rm{2}}.{\rm{5 }} \times {\rm{ 1}}{0^{-{\rm{4}}}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex} carries a current of 3.0 A. What is its associated magnetic moment?
questions.question_html: A closely wound solenoid of 800 turns and area of cross section {tex}{\rm{2}}.{\rm{5 }} \times {\rm{ 1}}{0^{-{\rm{4}}}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex} carries a current of 3.0 A. What is its associated magnetic moment?
questions.explanation: m = NIA = {tex}800\times 3\times 2.5\times 10^{-4}{/tex} = 0.6 J/T

questions.question_text: When a bar magnet is pushed towards the coil connected in series with a galvanometer, the pointer in the galvanometer G

options.html: shows deflection while bar is stationary

questions.correct_option_text: shows deflection while bar is in motion
questions.explanation: When bar is in motion then flux will changed hence current will be induced.

questions.question_text: m 2 V -1 s -1 is the SI unit of which of the following?
questions.question_html: m2V-1s-1 is the SI unit of which of the following?
questions.explanation: The charge carrier in most metals is the negatively charged electron. The mobility of the charge carrier is defined as the drift velocity of the charge carrier per unit electric field. It is denoted by {tex}\mu{/tex} and {tex}\mu=v_{d}/E{/tex} is given as. The Sl unit of {tex}\mu{/tex} is m2V-1s-1.

questions.question_text: Two condensers, one of capacity C and the other of capacity {tex}\frac{C}{2}{/tex} are connected to a V volt battery, as shown. The work done in charging fully both the condensers is:
questions.question_html: Two condensers, one of capacity C and the other of capacity {tex}\frac{C}{2}{/tex} are connected to a V volt battery, as shown. The work done in charging fully both the condensers is:
questions.explanation: The equivalent capacitance of the two condensers connected in parallel is {tex}C^{\prime}=C+\frac{C}{2}=\frac{3 C}{2}{/tex} The work done in charging both the capacitors is stored as potential energy. {tex}\therefore W=U=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{3 C}{2}\right) V^{2}=\frac{3}{4} C V^{2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A milliammeter of range 10 mA has a coil of resistance 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} .To use it as an ammeter of range 1 A, the required shunt must have a resistance of
questions.question_html: A milliammeter of range 10 mA has a coil of resistance 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex}.To use it as an ammeter of range 1 A, the required shunt must have a resistance of

questions.question_text: The magnetic potential energy stored in a certain inductor is 25 mJ, when the current in the inductor is 60 mA. This inductor is of inductance:
questions.question_html: The magnetic potential energy stored in a certain inductor is 25 mJ, when the current in the inductor is 60 mA. This inductor is of inductance:
questions.explanation: Energy stored in the inductor {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} L I^{2}{/tex} {tex}25 \times 10^{-3}=\frac{1}{2} \times L \times\left(60 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}L=\frac{25 \times 2 \times 10^{6} \times 10^{-3}}{3600}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{500}{36} \mathrm{H}{/tex} = 13.89 H

questions.question_text: A pair of adjacent coils has a mutual inductance of 1.5 H. If the current in one coil changes from 0 to 20 A in 0.5 s, change of flux linkage with the other coil is
questions.question_html: A pair of adjacent coils has a mutual inductance of 1.5 H. If the current in one coil changes from 0 to 20 A in 0.5 s, change of flux linkage with the other coil is
questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta \phi = M\Delta i = 1.5 \times 20 = 30{\rm{Wb}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A short bar magnet has a magnetic moment of 0.48 J/T. Magnetic field produced by the magnet at a distance of 10 cm from the centre of the magnet on the axis has a direction and magnitude of
questions.question_html: A short bar magnet has a magnetic moment of 0.48 J/T. Magnetic field produced by the magnet at a distance of 10 cm from the centre of the magnet on the axis has a direction and magnitude of
questions.explanation: {tex}\overrightarrow B_{axial} =\frac{\mu_o}{4\pi}\frac{2\overrightarrow m}{r^3}{/tex} {tex}=10^{-7}\times \frac{2\times 0.48}{0.1^{3}} = 960 \times 10^{-7} T = 0.96 G{/tex} Direction of magnetic field at axial point is along direction of magnetic moment i.e. from South to North

questions.question_text: A series LCR circuit with R = 20 {tex}\Omega {/tex} , L = 1.5 H and C = 35 {tex}\mu {\rm{F}}{/tex} is connected to a variable-frequency 200 V ac supply. When the frequency of the supply equals the natural frequency of the circuit, average power transferred to the circuit in one complete cycle is
questions.question_html: A series LCR circuit with R = 20 {tex}\Omega {/tex}, L = 1.5 H and C = 35 {tex}\mu {\rm{F}}{/tex} is connected to a variable-frequency 200 V ac supply. When the frequency of the supply equals the natural frequency of the circuit, average power transferred to the circuit in one complete cycle is
questions.explanation: When the frequency of the supply equals the natural frequency of the circuit, X L = X C In this case, impedance Z = R Average power, {tex}{P_{av}} = {V_{rms}} \times {i_{rms}} \times \cos \phi {/tex} {tex} = {V_{rms}} \times \frac{{{V_{rms}}}}{Z} \times \frac{R}{Z}{/tex} As, Z = R {tex}{P_{av}} = \frac{{{{\left( {{V_{rms}}} \right)}^2}}}{R} = \frac{{200 \times 200}}{{20}} = 2000W{/tex}

questions.question_text: Which of the following is responsible for the flow of current in a conductor?
questions.question_html: Which of the following is responsible for the flow of current in a conductor?
questions.explanation: In a conductor, the current is carried by free electrons.

questions.question_text: The current flowing through a lamp marked as 50 W, 250 V is:
questions.question_html: The current flowing through a lamp marked as 50 W, 250 V is:
questions.explanation: I = {tex}\frac{P}{V}=\frac{50 \mathrm{~W}}{250 \mathrm{~V}}{/tex} = 0.2 A

questions.question_text: A half ring of radius R has a charge per unit length equal to {tex}\lambda {/tex} . The field at the center is
questions.question_html: A half ring of radius R has a charge per unit length equal to {tex}\lambda {/tex}. The field at the center is
questions.explanation: the field at center is given by {tex}\frac{2\lambda}{4\pi\varepsilon_0R}{/tex}

questions.question_text: In a cyclotron, a charged particle
questions.question_html: In a cyclotron, a charged particle

options.text: speeds up between the dees because of the magnetic field.
options.html: speeds up between the dees because of the magnetic field.

options.text: slows down within a dee and speeds up between dees.
options.html: slows down within a dee and speeds up between dees.

questions.explanation: The electric field and magnetic field are perpendicular to each other. When a charge is placed between dees in empty space then it experiences a force due to the electric field and the magnetic field keeps it into a circular path. Inside dees, there is no electric field due to the shielding effect of charge or field only magnetic keeps the circular motion of charged particle inside. So inside dees charge moves with constant velocity. or In a cyclotron, a charged particle describes the circular paths inside the dees and is accelerated while going from one dee to another due to electric field. During circular path, the charged particle has centripetal acceleration which is provided by the magnetic force due to magnetic field. Therefore, a charged particle undergoes acceleration all the time, inside the cyclotron.

questions.question_text: A magnet of magnetic moment M is kept in a uniform magnetic field of strength B, making an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with its direction. The torque acting on it is:
questions.question_html: A magnet of magnetic moment M is kept in a uniform magnetic field of strength B, making an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with its direction. The torque acting on it is:
questions.explanation: Torque is cross product of magnetic moment and magnetic field. Therefore, magnitude of torque is given by {tex}MB\,\sin \theta{/tex}

questions.question_text: 0.2 F capacitor is charged to 600 V by a battery, on removing the battery, it is connected with another parallel plate condenser of 1 F. the potential decreases to:
questions.question_html: 0.2 F capacitor is charged to 600 V by a battery, on removing the battery, it is connected with another parallel plate condenser of 1 F. the potential decreases to:
questions.explanation: By conservation of charge, 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 600 = (0.2 + 1) V {tex}\therefore V=\frac{0.2 \times 600}{1.2}{/tex} = 100 V

questions.question_text: Law stating that force is directly proportional to the product of charges and inversely proportional to the square of the separation between them is called:
questions.question_html: Law stating that force is directly proportional to the product of charges and inversely proportional to the square of the separation between them is called:
questions.explanation: Coulomb's law states that, the magnitude of the electrostatic force of attraction or repulsion between two point charges is directly proportional to the product of the magnitudes of charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distances between them. The force acts always along the line joining the two charges.

questions.question_text: Instructions for Question 52 to 55: The Wheatstone bridge works on the principle of null deflection, i.e. the ratio of their resistances are equal and no current flows through the circuit. Under normal conditions, the bridge is in the unbalanced condition where current flows through the galvanometer. The bridge is said to be in a balanced condition when no current flows through the galvanometer. This condition can be achieved by adjusting the known resistance and variable resistance. The Wheatstone bridge is an arrangement of four resistances – R 1 , R 2 , R 3 , R 4 . The null-point condition is given by:
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 52 to 55:

The Wheatstone bridge works on the principle of null deflection, i.e. the ratio of their resistances are equal and no current flows through the circuit. Under normal conditions, the bridge is in the unbalanced condition where current flows through the galvanometer. The bridge is said to be in a balanced condition when no current flows through the galvanometer. This condition can be achieved by adjusting the known resistance and variable resistance.

 


The Wheatstone bridge is an arrangement of four resistances – R1, R2, R3, R4. The null-point condition is given by:
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac {R_1}{R_2} = \frac {R_3}{R_4}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Instructions for Question 52 to 55: The Wheatstone bridge works on the principle of null deflection, i.e. the ratio of their resistances are equal and no current flows through the circuit. Under normal conditions, the bridge is in the unbalanced condition where current flows through the galvanometer. The bridge is said to be in a balanced condition when no current flows through the galvanometer. This condition can be achieved by adjusting the known resistance and variable resistance. The Wheatstone bridge is used for the precise measurement of ________.
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 52 to 55:

The Wheatstone bridge works on the principle of null deflection, i.e. the ratio of their resistances are equal and no current flows through the circuit. Under normal conditions, the bridge is in the unbalanced condition where current flows through the galvanometer. The bridge is said to be in a balanced condition when no current flows through the galvanometer. This condition can be achieved by adjusting the known resistance and variable resistance.

 


The Wheatstone bridge is used for the precise measurement of ________.
questions.explanation: low resistance

questions.question_text: Instructions for Question 52 to 55: The Wheatstone bridge works on the principle of null deflection, i.e. the ratio of their resistances are equal and no current flows through the circuit. Under normal conditions, the bridge is in the unbalanced condition where current flows through the galvanometer. The bridge is said to be in a balanced condition when no current flows through the galvanometer. This condition can be achieved by adjusting the known resistance and variable resistance. Why are the connections between resistors in a Wheatstone or meter bridge made of thick copper strips?
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 52 to 55:

The Wheatstone bridge works on the principle of null deflection, i.e. the ratio of their resistances are equal and no current flows through the circuit. Under normal conditions, the bridge is in the unbalanced condition where current flows through the galvanometer. The bridge is said to be in a balanced condition when no current flows through the galvanometer. This condition can be achieved by adjusting the known resistance and variable resistance.

 


Why are the connections between resistors in a Wheatstone or meter bridge made of thick copper strips?
questions.explanation: Minimize the resistance

questions.question_text: Instructions for Question 52 to 55: The Wheatstone bridge works on the principle of null deflection, i.e. the ratio of their resistances are equal and no current flows through the circuit. Under normal conditions, the bridge is in the unbalanced condition where current flows through the galvanometer. The bridge is said to be in a balanced condition when no current flows through the galvanometer. This condition can be achieved by adjusting the known resistance and variable resistance. What happens if the galvanometer and cell are interchanged at the balance point of the bridge?
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 52 to 55:

The Wheatstone bridge works on the principle of null deflection, i.e. the ratio of their resistances are equal and no current flows through the circuit. Under normal conditions, the bridge is in the unbalanced condition where current flows through the galvanometer. The bridge is said to be in a balanced condition when no current flows through the galvanometer. This condition can be achieved by adjusting the known resistance and variable resistance.

 


What happens if the galvanometer and cell are interchanged at the balance point of the bridge?
questions.explanation: No deflection
